notes.anchor: acquitted of collusion by the tribuni aerarii
notes.note: Praevaricatio was the collusion of a prosecutor with the accused to throw a case. The tribuni aerarii formed the third decury of jurors (alongside senators and knights); here they alone voted to acquit Drusus, carrying it by four votes.

notes.anchor: I am to defend Vatinius the same day
notes.note: P. Vatinius, the Caesarian tribune of 59 BC whom Cicero had savagely attacked in 56; now, reconciled to the triumvirs, Cicero defends him --- a turn he found galling but politically unavoidable.
A little tragedy Quintus had written (titled after Sophocles' lost Syndeipnoi, on the heroes at Tenedos); Cicero, playing critic, says he gave it no approval at all.
Quintus was serving with Caesar on the second expedition to Britain in 54 BC; Cicero had dreaded the crossing of the Ocean but now relishes the literary subject the campaign offers his brother.
A Greek proverb (glaukas eis Athenas) for a pointless gift --- carrying owls to Athens, where Athena's owl was everywhere; Cicero means his verses would add nothing to so rich a subject.

notes.anchor: what does Caesar say about our verses
notes.note: Cicero had sent Caesar a poem (probably his epic on his own consulship or on Britain); Caesar praised the opening as better than anything in Greek but called a stretch slack, and Cicero anxiously wants Quintus's candid report.
